prompt:
The Foundry batch platform runs our long exports (warehouse pulls, statement archives) as async jobs, and every team that talks to it has hand-rolled the same poll loop — one of them with a bare time.Sleep(30 * time.Second) that makes its test suite take four minutes. I want the one true client: package statuspoll, statuspoll.go, stdlib only. statuspoll_test.go is the acceptance contract (scripted local mock of the platform, exact request/wait assertions); `go test -race -timeout 30s ./...` green and the test file untouched means done.

Their protocol, from the platform team's doc:
- Submit: POST {base}/jobs with JSON {"kind": <string>, "params": <object>} -> 202 Accepted with a Location header pointing at the job's status URL. Location may be absolute or relative — resolve relative ones against the submit URL. Anything other than a 202 is a refusal.
- Status: GET the status URL. While the job is working you get 200 {"state": "queued"} or {"state": "running"}. Success is signalled with a 303 See Other whose Location (again possibly relative) points at the result document. The other terminal answers are 200 {"state": "failed", "error": <detail>} and 200 {"state": "expired"} (results are purged after a retention window). Any state string you don't recognize: fail loudly with the state in the message.
- Result: GET the 303's target -> 200 with a JSON object; that object is the caller's answer.

API:
- `New(baseURL string, client *http.Client, opts Options) *Client`
- `type Options struct { Schedule []time.Duration; MaxPolls int; Sleep func(ctx context.Context, d time.Duration) error }`
- `Submit(ctx, kind string, params map[string]any) (*Job, error)` where `type Job struct { StatusURL string }` — callers persist that URL in the DB and resume waiting after a deploy, so
- `Wait(ctx, job *Job) (map[string]any, error)` must work from nothing but a Job, and
- `Run(ctx, kind, params)` = Submit then Wait.

Polling discipline (this is the part I care about):
- Fixed schedule, not header-driven: wait Schedule[0] before the FIRST poll (a 202 means the job just got queued; polling instantly is rude), Schedule[1] before the second, and when the schedule runs out keep repeating its last entry. Empty/nil Schedule means []time.Duration{time.Second}.
- Every wait goes through Options.Sleep. Nil Sleep defaults to a real timer that honors ctx — but nothing in this package may call time.Sleep directly, and if the sleeper returns an error (context died mid-wait), stop immediately and surface it so errors.Is sees the context error. The scripted tests inject a recording sleeper; a real sleep anywhere in the tested path is a bug.
- MaxPolls is the status-GET budget per Wait (<= 0 means 10). Budget gone while the job still isn't terminal -> *PollTimeoutError{Polls int, LastState string} with the count visible in Error().

Typed failures, all pointer errors reachable with errors.As: *APIError{Status int, Body string} for any protocol-level refusal (bad submit status, poll status we don't expect, failed result fetch — preserve the response body), *JobFailedError{Message string} carrying the platform's error detail, *JobExpiredError{}, *PollTimeoutError as above.

One trap the platform doc warns about and I'll repeat: Go's http.Client chases a 303 on its own and your poll will never see it — it'll just hand you the result document with a 200 while you think you're reading a status. The acceptance tests inject a stock client, so the client itself must make sure redirects reach the poll loop unfollowed (copy-and-adjust the client, don't mutate the caller's).
